content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
December 1,
1993
Philip D.
Kohn
Laguna Beach City Attorney
Rutan
& Tucker
Bank of the West,
Suite 1400
P.O.
Box 1950
Costa Mesa,
CA
92628—1950
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-93--447
Dear Mr.
Kohn:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice with
respect to the duties of the following Laguna Beach officials:
City Councilmember Robert Gentry,
City Manager Kenneth Frank,
Deputy City Manager/Director of Recreation and Social Services
Cynthia King,
and Planning Commissioner Jeffrey Powers, under the
conflict-of—interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the
“Act”).
1
-
QUESTION
May officials who lost their homes to the October 27,
1993,
firestorm participate in decisions regarding fire recovery
efforts?
CONCLUS ION
Since the “public generally” exception of Regulation 18703
applies to your facts,
Councilmember Gentry,
City Manager Frank,
Deputy City Manager/Director of Recreation and Social Services
King,
and Planning Commissioner Powers may participate in
decisions regarding fire recovery so long as none of the decisions
in question will confer a special or unique benefit on any of the
public officials in comparison to the decisions’ affect on the
interests of other persons similarly affected by the firestorm.
1
Government Code Sections
81000—91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Sections
18000-18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A—93—447
Page 2
FACTS
You have requested expedited advice as City Attorney of the
City of Laguna Beach on behalf of City Councilmember Robert
Gentry, City Manager Kenneth Frank,
Deputy City Manager/Director
of Recreation and Social Services Cynthia King and Planning
Commission Jeffrey Powers.
Each named official lost their primary
residence in the October 27,
1993, firestorm.
In addition,
Councilmember Gentry also lost a home owned for rental purposes.
You stated that approximately 270 homes within the city
limits and over 100 other homes near the city boundaries were
destroyed in the firestoriu.
In light of the extensive losses,
a
proclamation of local emergency was declared by the city on
October 27,
1993.
You stated that on the same day,
a state of
emergency was proclaimed by Governor Pete Wilson and President
Bill Clinton declared the city a national disaster area.
You have asked whether city officials who lost homes in the
firestorm could participate in decisions related to fire recovery.
You stated that those efforts are expected to include, but are not
limited to,
consideration of regulations for rebuilding of
residences,
debris cleanup,
erosion control and re—seeding,
reconstruction of public facilities and undergrounding of
utilities.
You also stated that none of the decisions in question will
confer a special or unique benefit on any of the public officials,
in comparison to the decisions’ affect on the interests of other
persons similarly affected by the firestorm.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making,
or otherwise using the official’s
position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest.
Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public enera1ly,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on any real
property in which the public official has a direct or indirect
interest worth $1,000 or more.
(Section 87103(b).)
Thus, the
officials in question would be prohibited from participating in or


Our File No. A-93-447
Page 3
influencing any decision that will have a reasonably foreseeable
2
and material
3 financial effect on their homes.
However,
you have asked specifically about the “public
generally” exception of the Act.
Regulation 18703 provides an
exception to the conflict—of-interest provisions if the effect on
the official’s interest is not distinguishable from the effect on
the public generally.
For the “public generally” exception to
apply,
a decision must affect the official’s interests in
substantially the same manner as it would affect a significant
seqment of the public.
(Regulation 18703.)
On September 7,
1993, the Commission adopted specific
standards to determine when a govermnental decision will fall
within the “public generally” exception.
New Regulation 18703(c)
provides:
[T]he financial effect of a governmental
decision on an official is indistinguishable from
its financial effect on the public generally if
both of the following apply:
(1)
The decision will affect an economic
interest of the official, other than an
economic interest as defined in Section
87103(e),
in substantially the same manner as
other persons subject to a state of emergency,
proclaimed by the Governor pursuant to
Government Code Section 8625,
or proclaimed by
the governing body of a city or county.
(2)
The decision is required to mitigate
against the effects directly arising out of
the emergency,
and strict adherence to the Act
will prevent, hinder, or delay the mitigation
of the effects of the emergency.
2
Whether the financial effects of a decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time a governmental decisioi is made depends on
the facts of each particular case.
An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that
it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (
re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)
The Commission has adopted various regulations which contain
several objective standards for determining whether the effect of
a decision will be material,
depending on the nature of the
decision and economic interest involved.
(Regulation 18702.)
The
applicable standards with respect to real property interests are
contained in Regulations 18702.1 and Regulation 18702.3
(copies
enclosed.)


Our File No.
A-93-447
Page 4
You stated that on October 27,
1993,
in response to the
firestorm, the City of Laguna Beach issued a proclamation of local
emergency.
In addition,
on that same date,
a state of emergency
was proclaimed by Governor Pete Wilson and President Bill Clinton
declared the city a national disaster area.
You also stated that the city is now acting on fire recovery
iueasures.
You stated that those efforts are expected to include
the consideration of regulations for rebuilding of residences,
debris cleanup, erosion control and re—seeding, reconstruction of
public facilities and undergrounding of utilities.
Finally, you also stated that approximately 270 homes within
the city limits were destroyed in the firestorm.
City
Councilmember Robert Gentry, City Manager Kenneth Frank,
Deputy
City Manager/Director of Recreation and Social Services Cynthia
King and Planning Commission Jeffrey Powers each lost their
primary residence in the firestorm.
You stated that the decisions
regarding the city’s recovery efforts will affect the officials’
economic interests in substantially the same manner as other
persons suffering loses in the firestorm.
4
Consequently,
based on
your facts, the “public generally” exception applies and the
officials may participate in the decisions.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—590l.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
1 Counsel
By: ‘John W. Wallace
Counsel,
Legal Division
We note that you stated that Councilmember Gentry lost two
homes in the fire, the councilmember’s primary residence and a
residence owned for rental purposes.
However,
based on the nature
of the decisions in question, it appears that the councilmember’s
interests will be affected in substantially the same manner as
other persons who suffered losses in the firestorm.
(Marsh Advice
Letter,
No.
1-90-151;
Newton Advice Letter,
No. A-92-328.)
Thus,
the councilmember may participate in fire recovery decisions
affecting both his residence and rental property.
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of l0 per page.

A—93—447
